questionText: Prove by exhaustion that for every integer $n$, $n^2$ leaves a remainder of $0$ or $1$ when divided by $3$.

steps.description: Choose an exhaustive split by residue mod 3.
steps.explanation: Every integer leaves remainder $0$, $1$ or $2$ on division by $3$. Checking these three cases is exhaustive.
A multiple of $3$ has the form $3k$; we square it to find the remainder of $n^2$.
Squaring $3k$ gives $9k^2$, which is $3$ times an integer, so the remainder is $0$.
An integer one more than a multiple of $3$ has the form $3k+1$.
Expanding and grouping the multiple of $3$ shows the remainder is $1$.
An integer two more than a multiple of $3$ has the form $3k+2$.
Expanding gives $9k^2+12k+4$; grouping the multiple of $3$ leaves remainder $1$ (since $4=3+1$).
The three cases give remainders $0$, $1$ and $1$, so only $0$ or $1$ ever occurs.
Every integer falls into one of the three residue classes, so no case is missing.
By checking all cases, the square of any integer leaves remainder $0$ or $1$ when divided by $3$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: For $n=3k,\,3k+1,\,3k+2$ the squares are $3(3k^2),\ 3(3k^2+2k)+1,\ 3(3k^2+4k+1)+1$, giving remainders $0,1,1$. Hence $n^2\equiv0$ or $1\pmod3$. $\blacksquare$
